what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard request that captures and preserves earlier ChatGPT research about OpenAI Workspace Agents so Claude Code members can read it without relying on hidden chat history.
observations: It records three official source URLs checked on 2026-06-23.
It states Workspace Agents are ChatGPT workspace-side agents triggered by backend systems through a published API channel.
It distinguishes Workspace Agents from Claude Code, Codex, and Company/RHM.
It gives the trigger endpoint pattern POST https://api.chatgpt.com/v1/workspace_agents/{id}/trigger with an agtch_XXX id.
It notes authentication uses a Workspace Agent access token as a bearer token.
It lists five design implications for Company, centered on return-path and edge-worker use.
It poses seven questions for Claude Code members about availability, publishing, tools, write-back, result retrieval, auth posture, and System A representation.
It recommends a minimal experiment: create a triggerable agent that writes back to a safe Company channel or board item before building scheduled/inbox watcher patterns.
standouts: The central design issue is the return path: triggering is easy, but the agent must write back into Vi/fabric through a known connector.
Workspace Agents are framed as ChatGPT-side edge workers or bridge participants, not as the Company core.
The file pushes for a minimal write-back proof before any scheduled or inbox-watcher pattern.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter convention with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, thread, links, created, updated, and history.
Uses markdown headings, bullet lists, and a numbered list for structured argumentation.
Cites external official sources with checked dates.
concerns: The claims depend on external documentation that is not reproduced in the file.
All listed questions remain unanswered and the item state is still open.
Some terms like Vi/fabric and Company MCP connector are used without definition in this file.
notes: A research pack asking Claude Code members to verify whether OpenAI Workspace Agents can be published, triggered, and made to write back into Company fabric before treating them as integration surfaces.
questions: Were the listed official URLs actually reachable and current on 2026-06-23?
What exactly do Vi and fabric refer to in this context?
What is the Company MCP connector mentioned as a possible write-back path?
Has Tim's ChatGPT Business workspace been confirmed to support API-triggerable Workspace Agents?
Was the recommended minimal write-back experiment performed?
Is there a result retrieval API in addition to agent-initiated write-back?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the stated purpose is to capture earlier ChatGPT research for incoming Claude Code members, and the file is exactly that kind of sourced research summary with questions and recommendations.
apparent_themes: ChatGPT-side agents as edge workers rather than core fabric
intention_signals: It tries to introduce OpenAI Workspace Agents as a candidate integration point for Company, define their subordinate role relative to the Company core, and prompt the team to validate availability and a working write-back path before designing workflows around them.
novelty: It explicitly treats a ChatGPT platform agent surface as a possible external edge worker for a Claude Code-centered system and makes the return-path problem the central design question rather than the trigger mechanism.
contribution: It contributes a dated, sourced summary of Workspace Agent API semantics, a clear boundary statement separating them from Company core, concrete validation questions, and a minimal experiment plan focused on proving write-back.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard research pack summarizing OpenAI Workspace Agent trigger semantics and arguing that the key integration problem for Company is designing a reliable return path for agent output back into fabric.
intention_for_embedding: To preserve ChatGPT research for Claude Code members and guide them to validate Workspace Agent availability and write-back before building team-facing workflows.
